goal: Enable security teams to run active, multi-channel social engineering campaigns driven by AI-adaptive AEP chatbots, review employee conversation responses, and classify behavioral outcomes — going beyond passive click/submit simulation to real adversarial tradecraft where the attacker adapts to each employee's behavior in real time.
aep_definition: An AEP (Adversary Emulation Pathway) is an AI-driven scenario-based chatbot that conducts social engineering interactions autonomously and adapts its responses based on how the target employee replies. Defined by a scenario prompt (attacker persona, goal, tactics). Example: a bribe-offer AEP escalates if the employee shows curiosity, tries a different angle if they resist. Admins create AEPs by writing a scenario prompt, testing in a live chat simulator, and iterating with refinement prompts until satisfied.
success_metrics: Admin can build a novel multi-channel attack sequence without engineering support
Admin can configure and submit a campaign for approval in under 15 minutes
All employee conversation threads are surfaced and reviewable within the platform
Complicity classification is completed within 48 hours of campaign close
Risk scores update within 60 seconds of complicity marking
constraints: Multi-channel orchestration (Voice, WhatsApp, Viber) may require external provider integrations not yet confirmed
AEP is an AI-driven adaptive chatbot (branching/dynamic by nature) — LLM selection, hosting, latency SLA, and prompt injection guardrails are unconfirmed
AEP builder (prompt editor + chat simulator) is a new product surface with no DS precedent
Node-based editor complexity must be bounded to linear sequences in v1 (attack sequences, not AEPs)
Compliance/RBAC for complicity marking must be defined before conversation management UI is built
'Request start' approval flow requires a new approver role not present in current Dune RBAC model
Navigation home for the five sections must be resolved before any individual screen is designed
Existing smishing simulation (sms-phishing feature) must remain functional and not be disrupted
assumptions: AEPs are AI chatbots defined by scenario prompts — adaptive, not linear scripts
AEP authoring uses a prompt editor + live chat simulator (test and iterate loop)
Dune-seeded AEP library ships at launch (bribe, authority impersonation, urgency, curiosity baiting, reciprocity scenarios)
Customer AEP authoring is a planned capability — not confirmed for initial launch but architecture must accommodate it
Attack sequences are multi-channel but the orchestration model (automated vs. human-executed) is unconfirmed
Complicity marking is a judgment-based admin action, not a fully automated system event
Red Team Dashboard is already designed and should be documented, not redesigned
The node-based editor is for the attack library only, not for VEP authoring
Audience selection, scheduling, phone coverage, and remediation agent patterns carry over from smishing
gaps: AEP LLM architecture undefined — which model, hosted where, latency SLA, prompt injection guardrails
AEP prompt quality guardrails undefined — what prevents admins from writing harmful, discriminatory, or legally risky scenario prompts
AEP authoring scope unclear — is custom AEP creation available at initial launch or is it a planned future capability?
Complicity has no operational definition — auto-flagged or manually marked, binary or graduated
Approver role for campaign start requests is undefined — new RBAC role needed
Hybrid channel orchestration model (automated vs. operator-executed) is unconfirmed
Employee reply threading model for Voice and WhatsApp is undefined
Risk scoring extension for 'complicit' outcome is undefined
'Allowed sending days' interaction with approval flow timing is unspecified
Complicity marking reversal — permissions and audit trail requirements not defined
edge_cases: Attack node fails mid-sequence — does sequence continue on next channel or abort?
Campaign approved but start date has passed — activation grace window or auto-cancel?
Pending approval campaign with no active approvers
AEP generates a response that is off-script, offensive, or legally risky — guardrail and admin override needed
AEP enters a conversation loop with no resolution (employee keeps engaging but never tips into complicity signal)
Hybrid channel unavailable at send time — fallback behavior undefined
Conversation thread with no employee reply — passive non-engagement data point
Complicity flag auto-raised but admin has not yet reviewed — pending state visibility
Bulk complicity review — 50+ conversations with no bulk action defined
Employee responds in non-English — VEP assumes English
Admin edits VEP while active campaign is using it
Admin deletes attack sequence used by a scheduled campaign
Approver rejects campaign start request — admin notification and draft return
Admin marks complicit and then reverses — reversal workflow undefined

critical_questions.question: Which LLM powers AEP conversations, where is it hosted, what is the expected response latency, and how are prompt injections from employee replies prevented?
Is customer AEP authoring available at initial launch, or is it a planned post-launch capability? If post-launch, what is the estimated timeline?
What guardrails exist for AEP prompt content — how does Dune prevent admins from writing harmful, discriminatory, or legally risky scenario prompts?
What is the operational definition of 'complicit'? Auto-flagged by system (keyword/NLP), manually flagged by a human operator, or a combination?
Who is the approver for campaign start requests? New RBAC role, designated second admin, or automated compliance gate? What is the approval SLA?
For hybrid channel attacks, does Dune orchestrate channel transitions directly, or does the VEP guide a human operator who executes the steps?
How are employee replies threaded back to a campaign for Voice and WhatsApp channels?
How does the 'complicit' outcome map to risk scoring? Does it replace or augment the smishing click/submit graduated model?
Are VEPs authored by Dune only, by customers, or both? If customer-authored, what are the guardrails for harmful conversation scripts?
Is Red Team a separate nav section from Simulations → Smishing, or does it expand Simulations? Navigation architecture must be settled before screen design.
What does 'allowed sending days' interact with in the approval flow? If approved on Monday but only allowed Tuesday/Thursday, does it auto-start on next allowed day?
Can complicity marking be reversed once set? Who has permission to reverse, and what is the audit trail requirement?
For voice channel nodes: does Dune initiate the call, play a script, and transcribe the response? Or is voice a human-executed step that the admin logs manually?
What is the long-term relationship between Red Team and the existing smishing simulation — separate products, separate sections, or eventual absorption?

design_risks: AEP prompt authoring is a novel skill — without a live chat simulator for testing, admins cannot assess AEP quality before deployment; simulator is essential, not optional
AEP off-script risk — AI-generated responses may be offensive, legally risky, or accidentally realistic; content guardrails and admin override mechanism are required
Node-based editor scope creep — without v1 scope constraint (linear sequences only), editor will consume disproportionate effort
'Complicit' as legal/HR exposure — label may cause misuse or distress; softer terminology alternatives should be evaluated
Approval workflow dead-end — no escalation path if approver is inactive creates indefinite pending campaigns
Conversation management at scale — 200+ threads without bulk actions and smart sorting is unusable
Multi-channel availability gaps — WhatsApp/Viber require business account setup; admins may build attacks they cannot send
teaching_notes: AEP = AI chatbot, not a script. An AEP holds a persona (who), goal (what), and tactics (how it adapts). Admin creates by writing a scenario prompt, not by scripting conversation nodes.
AEP builder UX = prompt editor + live chat simulator side-by-side. Admin plays the role of target employee in the simulator to validate AEP behavior before activation.

AEP library (Dune-seeded) follows same browse/create/clone pattern as smishing template library (knowledge/features/sms-phishing/design-strategy.md Screen 1), but preview surface is a live demo chat, not a static script.
Node-based editor: keep minimal in v1 — nodes are channel steps, edges are sequence order; canvas is read-only in campaign creation
Request-start campaign model is new — analogous to change request/CAB approval flow; key requirements: clear pending state, approver notification, graceful rejection path
Complicit marking is judgment-based (admin decides), not event-based (system records) — design for inter-rater clarity with inline guidance at the point of marking
Graduated risk model must be extended for red team complicit outcome — confirm weighting with PM before designing campaign detail and reporting UI
Navigation architecture must be resolved first — five sections need a nav home before any individual screen design begins
